name: Synthetic Demographic Variants Are Used for a Fairness Stress-test Pilot
statement: Walsh et al. report an early fairness pilot in which LLM evaluations of 100 original Casper responses were compared with 100 analogous synthetic responses containing demographic information; the reported average score deltas were approximately zero, with larger-sample fairness analysis still needed.
population_or_context: Open-response SJT automated scoring for Casper admissions assessment responses.
time_horizon: Frontiers in Education article published on 2026-04-28 and retrieved on 2026-06-02.
quantitative_note: The fairness pilot uses 100 original and 100 analogous synthetic responses; the article reports near-zero average score deltas for the inspected demographic comparisons.
caveats: The source describes the fairness analysis as a pilot.
Small sample size limits between-group effect-size interpretation.
Open-response SJT admissions scoring is not the same construct as classroom correctness scoring.
evaluation_method: Paired comparison of automated LLM evaluations on original responses and analogous demographic-injected synthetic responses.
reported_metrics: Average score deltas for gender and race comparisons; broader study reports automated-scoring performance against human ratings.
limitations: The synthetic responses are used for fairness probing, not as an independently human-labeled validation set.
Further validation is required before generalizing across demographic groups, tasks, or scoring rubrics.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase_note: The staged finding paraphrases the original-versus-analogous synthetic response fairness pilot and its sample-size caveat.
